source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, $AB \parallel CD$, $\angle FGB = 155^\circ$, $FG$ bisects $\angle EFD$, what is the size of $\angle BEF$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} Since $AB\parallel CD$ and $\angle FGB=155^\circ$,\\
it follows that $\angle BEF+\angle EFD=180^\circ$,\\
hence $\angle GFD=180^\circ-\angle FGB=180^\circ-155^\circ=25^\circ$,\\
Given that $FG$ bisects $\angle EFD$,\\
it implies $\angle EFD=2\angle GFD=2\times 25^\circ=50^\circ$,\\
thus $\angle BEF=180^\circ-\angle EFD=180^\circ-50^\circ=\boxed{130^\circ}$.

The question says "As shown in the figure" and uses geometric objects/relations (`AB || CD`, points E/F/G/B) whose configuration is not fully recoverable from text alone
This is a geometry angle-chasing task involving spatial arrangement and line intersections, which typically has strong visual dependence
multi_step: The provided answer uses multiple linked inferences: derive an angle from parallel-line geometry, use the angle bisector condition, then compute a final supplementary angle
The question combines at least two conditions (`AB || CD` and `FG` bisects `∠EFD`) before reaching the target angle
The task is not simple lookup; it requires sequential geometric reasoning over several intermediate angles
verifiability: The target is a single numeric angle measure, so correctness is easy to check against the final result
The answer text gives an explicit derivation ending in `130°`, which supports process-level checking
`asset_registry_record.registry_passed=true` and both question/answer are present, so the sample is structurally usable for verification
